caveats: a: The BLS table is May 2023 and measures occupations rather than tasks.
a: Radio groups increasingly sell digital services whose labor mix may not be fully represented in the station occupation table.
a: The injected labor-intensity value is inherited from ancestor sector 51 and is LOW quality, so it may misstate radio stations' compensation-to-receipts structure.
rho: The survey reports manager responses and does not audit implementation or savings.
rho: The phrase roughly half combines current users with stations merely planning adoption.
rho: Survey participants may be more digitally engaged than the full station population.
e: The NAICS definition establishes broadcasting activity but not commercial status, profitability, independence, or transaction size.
e: FCC approval makes license transfers possible but does not show the fraction that is economically eligible.
e: The firm-count input n is a declared dataset gap and must remain MISSING; this estimate does not replace or infer n.
s5: FCC transfer rules establish process, not historical seller incidence.
s5: A license transfer may involve a single station, a cluster, or a broader company and therefore does not map cleanly to firms.
s5: Distressed station transfers may not represent recurring positive-EBITDA acquisition opportunities.
q: Neither source directly measures the share of labor savings retained in station EBITDA.
q: Digital revenue growth can require third-party product costs and additional specialist labor.
q: Local market concentration, ratings, format, and advertiser mix produce wide station-level variation.
d5: Spot-ad revenue is nominal and does not directly measure constant-price service demand.
d5: S&P's spot forecasts exclude some digital and non-spot station revenue.
d5: Audio listening shares measure time, not advertiser spending or station profitability.
d5: Political advertising creates material cycle effects.

o: Local news, talk, sports, and emergency-oriented formats may persist more strongly than music formats.
o: A station can remain licensed while much programming and production is centralized elsewhere.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Stations aggregate listener attention and sell recurring local and national advertising, sponsorship, political advertising, digital marketing, streaming inventory, events, and related services. Revenue is externally paid and often repeat, but cyclical, audience-sensitive, and exposed to advertiser migration across media.
lens.excluded_activities: Noncommercial and public stations without a commercial revenue model, internet-only audio services without terrestrial station operations, podcast-only businesses, networks without station operations, and businesses whose principal activity is advertising agency work rather than radio broadcasting.
lens.included_activities: Commercial radio broadcasting stations serving external listeners and advertisers through over-the-air programming and station-operated streaming, digital advertising, sponsorship, and related recurring local marketing services, viewed at lower-middle-market EBITDA scale.

a.bridge: BLS occupation shares within Radio Broadcasting Stations are translated into a wage-weighted estimate of five-year AI-addressable labor tasks. Generative AI is applied to content preparation, voice tracking, transcription, editing, scheduling, ad copy, prospecting, sales support, traffic, and administration, while live personality, original reporting, local relationships, engineering, compliance, and final editorial accountability remain less addressable. Occupations are broader than tasks, so the bridge can overstate substitution within creative roles and understate routine work inside management and sales.
caveats: The BLS table is May 2023 and measures occupations rather than tasks.
Radio groups increasingly sell digital services whose labor mix may not be fully represented in the station occupation table.
The injected labor-intensity value is inherited from ancestor sector 51 and is LOW quality, so it may misstate radio stations' compensation-to-receipts structure.
a.change_evidence: A station-level task and time study measuring actual AI-assisted hours across programming, news, sales, production, engineering, and administration, segmented by market and station size, would materially change this estimate.
a.rationale: BLS reports that 53.21% of radio-station employment is in arts, design, entertainment, sports, and media, including 27.24% announcers and radio DJs; sales accounts for 18.65% and office and administrative support 10.33%. Many production, copy, research, scheduling, sales-support, and back-office tasks are AI-addressable, but local authenticity, live performance, reporting, client relationships, broadcast engineering, and regulated decision-making limit full exposure.

d5.bridge: S&P Global's nominal forecasts for national and core local radio spot revenue through 2030 are translated into a five-year constant-price service-demand multiplier and widened to include station digital revenue and audience trends. Nominal spot revenue can overstate real demand when ad prices rise, while excluding fast-growing digital services can understate total station demand; the range reflects both opposing errors.
caveats: Spot-ad revenue is nominal and does not directly measure constant-price service demand.
S&P's spot forecasts exclude some digital and non-spot station revenue.
Audio listening shares measure time, not advertiser spending or station profitability.
Political advertising creates material cycle effects.
d5.change_evidence: A constant-price forecast of total commercial radio-station output combining terrestrial, streaming, digital marketing, sponsorship, and events, with cohort-level listener trends, would materially change this multiplier.
d5.rationale: Terrestrial spot advertising faces persistent decline: S&P Global projected national radio spot revenue down 5% in 2025 and 3.5% in 2026, with core local spot also declining and both lower by 2030. Radio retains 66% of U.S. ad-supported audio time, but only 47% among ages 18 to 34 versus 73% among listeners 35 and older, implying ongoing demographic pressure. Digital station revenue and local relevance partly offset the decline, supporting a base below one but not a collapse case.

caveats: The NAICS definition establishes broadcasting activity but not commercial status, profitability, independence, or transaction size.
FCC approval makes license transfers possible but does not show the fraction that is economically eligible.
The firm-count input n is a declared dataset gap and must remain MISSING; this estimate does not replace or infer n.
e.change_evidence: A station-owner census linking commercial status, independent control, cluster structure, revenue, EBITDA, and lower-middle-market size would materially change this estimate.
e.rationale: Many commercial radio stations have external recurring advertiser relationships, transferable operating assets and licenses, and established local cash flows consistent with the default lens. The eligible share is reduced by noncommercial stations, very small or distressed operators, stations embedded in larger clusters, owner-dependent operations, weak or negative EBITDA, and entities for which a standalone station is not the appropriate transaction unit.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 51 ($506,769,522,547) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 51 ($2,139,449,301,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 51. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1728 (margins.json[51]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Information Services EBITDA/Sales 17.28% (conservative sector default)); revenue/employee $588,729 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 51); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: The estimate concerns the need for an operating entity, not preservation of current station employment.
Local news, talk, sports, and emergency-oriented formats may persist more strongly than music formats.
A station can remain licensed while much programming and production is centralized elsewhere.
o.change_evidence: Evidence on station closures, license surrender, format automation, consolidation, local staffing, and advertiser migration after deployment of AI audio and sales tools would materially change this estimate.
o.rationale: An accountable station operator remains necessary to hold and comply with FCC licenses, run transmitters and studios, curate local programming, manage emergency and public-interest obligations, maintain advertiser relationships, and protect the station brand. Operator persistence is weakened by audience migration to streaming and podcasts, syndicated or automated programming, network consolidation, direct digital advertising, and AI-generated audio that can reduce the distinctiveness of some formats.

caveats: Neither source directly measures the share of labor savings retained in station EBITDA.
Digital revenue growth can require third-party product costs and additional specialist labor.
Local market concentration, ratings, format, and advertiser mix produce wide station-level variation.
q.change_evidence: Station-level evidence comparing AI-driven cost reductions with subsequent ad rates, gross margins, advertiser churn, digital-service costs, audience delivery, and reinvestment would materially change this estimate.
q.rationale: Stations can retain some AI-enabled savings through local audience relationships, recurring advertiser accounts, scarce licenses, bundled campaigns, and differentiated personalities. Retention is constrained by rate competition, agency and advertiser bargaining, measurable digital alternatives, declining core broadcast revenue, the need to reinvest in streaming and digital products, and the risk that advertisers use AI directly for copy and targeting.

rho.bridge: A 2024 Borrell/RAB survey of 221 radio managers reporting that roughly half were already using generative AI or planning to do so is converted into the five-year fraction of addressable station tasks realized. The estimate gives more weight to actual-use than planning and allows adoption to expand over five years, while discounting for small-station resources, policy, copyright, trust, workflow integration, and local-brand constraints. Manager intent may overstate implemented, durable labor savings.
caveats: The survey reports manager responses and does not audit implementation or savings.
The phrase roughly half combines current users with stations merely planning adoption.
Survey participants may be more digitally engaged than the full station population.
rho.change_evidence: A representative longitudinal survey separating current production use from plans and linking each use case to audited time or cost savings at U.S. radio stations would materially change this range.
rho.rationale: The radio-manager survey shows adoption moving beyond experimentation: roughly half reported current use or plans, 91% had discussed AI, and 39% had established a best-practice policy. These indicators support material five-year realization in sales, production, and administration, but discussion and planning are not deployment, and stations face reputational, editorial, rights, and implementation constraints.

caveats: FCC transfer rules establish process, not historical seller incidence.
A license transfer may involve a single station, a cluster, or a broader company and therefore does not map cleanly to firms.
Distressed station transfers may not represent recurring positive-EBITDA acquisition opportunities.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year FCC transaction panel matching completed control transfers to a stable denominator of eligible commercial lower-middle-market radio owners, with pro forma transfers and internal reorganizations removed, would materially change this range.
s5.rationale: Radio stations and station groups are transferable, but sales require FCC applications and prior public-interest approval for substantial changes in ownership or control. Mature ownership structures and terrestrial revenue pressure can create sellers, while regulatory process, cluster ownership, financing, valuation gaps, and distress can suppress completed transactions. No suitable eligible-firm denominator was available, so the range remains a conservative judgment rather than a transaction-rate observation.

principal_driver: A large combined share of media production, sales, and administrative work is addressable by AI, with surveyed radio managers already moving from discussion toward use and policy formation.
principal_weakness: Demand for core terrestrial advertising is structurally weak and younger-listener share is lower, while the missing lower-middle-market firm count makes total target availability unmeasurable.
